_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Rankin County, MS. Formed in 1828 from Hinds County. Brandon is the county seat. Rankin County lies east of Jackson across the Pearl River and served as the eastern counterpart to the Hinds County plantation economy. The county's records survive well from the late 1820s, with no major courthouse fire.

previous_courthouses.location: Brandon, MS — early frame courthouse on the town square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county grew. Records survived the transition.

previous_courthouses.location: Brandon, MS — brick courthouse on the square
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current 1928 building. No catastrophic fire or war destruction. Records survived intact.

record_losses.cause: No major courthouse fire or war destruction
record_losses.description: Rankin County has not experienced a major courthouse fire or war-related record destruction. The Civil War disrupted record-keeping during the Vicksburg Campaign and the burning of Jackson across the river, but Rankin County itself was not systematically targeted. The courthouse in Brandon survived intact. Records run continuously from county formation in 1828, making Rankin County one of the better-preserved counties in the cluster for genealogical research.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Rankin County after 1828, the records almost certainly survive. Across the Pearl River, Jackson burned twice — but Brandon and its courthouse were spared. The genealogical challenge in Rankin County is not missing records but the nature of the records themselves: small farmers leave thinner paper trails than planters, and the eastern hill country was yeoman territory.
record_losses.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation.
deeds.repository: Rankin County Chancery Clerk, Brandon; Mississippi Department of Archives and History
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited MDAH digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records survive from near-formation.
wills_estates.gaps: Early loose papers are fragmentary but bound volumes are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Rankin County Chancery Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from county formation.
marriages.gaps: Minimal. Some early bonds show wear.
marriages.repository: Rankin County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.completeness: Good — court minutes survive from county formation.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor disruption during Civil War period, but no destruction.
court_minutes.repository: Rankin County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

vital_records.completeness: Mississippi statewide registration began 1912. Rural compliance was slow in the early years.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Vital Records

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1830-1940. Slave schedules (1850, 1860). Agricultural schedules document the smaller-scale farming economy east of Jackson.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Census records are essential baseline documentation. Agricultural schedules reveal whether an ancestor was a planter, a yeoman farmer, or a tenant — a distinction that shaped every aspect of life in Rankin County.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Rankin County, MS.

substitute_sources.description: Baptist and Methodist churches dominated Rankin County's religious landscape. Church minute books, membership rolls, and baptism records supplement civil records.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; Mississippi Baptist Historical Commission; Mississippi Conference United Methodist Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records are valuable for documenting families between census years. Baptist church discipline records provide detail about individuals that civil records do not. Church transfer letters document migration.

substitute_sources.description: Bureau records for Rankin County document labor contracts and marriage registrations for formerly enslaved families. The proximity to Jackson meant the Bureau's Rankin County operations were administered from the state capital.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracing Black families through emancipation. Labor contracts name individuals and often identify former slaveholders.
substitute_sources.source: Freedmen and Southern Society Project. Freedom: A Documentary History of Emancipation. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

narrative_hooks: Jackson burned twice across the river, but Brandon and the Rankin County courthouse were spared. If {ancestor_name} lived east of the Pearl River, {pronoun_possessive} records survived a war that destroyed the state capital within sight of the county line.

Rankin County's records run unbroken from 1828 — and if {ancestor_name} bought land, married, or appeared in court, the documentation is almost certainly in Brandon. The absence of a record here is more likely to reflect the ancestor's circumstances than the courthouse's losses.

The Choctaw people ceded the land that became Rankin County just five years before the county was organized. When {ancestor_name} arrived in the late 1820s or 1830s, {pronoun_subject} was settling land whose previous occupants were being marched westward on a trail of broken promises.

Rankin County was yeoman country — smaller farms, fewer enslaved people, and a thinner paper trail than the river counties to the west. If {ancestor_name} was a small farmer here, the deed book and the census may be the only records that document {pronoun_possessive} life.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.
National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Rankin County, MS.
Freedmen and Southern Society Project. Freedom: A Documentary History of Emancipation. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
